original_abstract: Nitride phosphors are suitable for white light-emitting diode applications. In this study, the structure of phosphor has been modified through cation substitution to induce charge variation and a rearrangement of neighboring nitride clusters, and consequently enhance its luminescent behavior. Substitution of Ca(2+) by Sr(2+) cations expanded the lattice volume and the bc plane, but shortened the distance between the layers along the a axis. Lattice distortion of the framework introduced high-coordination sites in the Sr/Eu centers and adequate space, thereby facilitating charge variation of activators under reduced atmosphere, as detected through X-ray absorption near-edge structure spectroscopy. As such, the photoluminescent intensity of the phosphors increased by more than 10% and a blue shift occurred. The microstructures of the samples were also analyzed using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy. Cation substitution induced a special change in the anion environment, as indicated in the solid-state Raman spectra. Moreover, typical ordering variations in the SiN4 and AlN4 clusters are generated in the lattice. Meanwhile, neighbor sequence of (Si/Al)N4 around the divalent centers were observed through solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy. The modified ordering distribution resulted in a rigid structure and improved the thermal quenching behavior. Thermal stability has been enhanced by 10% at 473 K when x = 0.9 in SrxCa0.993-xAlSiN3:Eu(2+)0.007 compared with that at x = 0. This study promotes the research of neighbor sequence for selective tetrahedral sites such as Li, Mg, Al, and Si coordinated by N atoms in contact with cation sites.
humanized_abstract: Cation exchange effects on nitride phosphors for green light-emitting diodes and white light-emitting diodes Cathodoluminescence and photoluminescence (PL) characteristics of SrxCa0.993-xAlSiN3:Eu2+ (x=0.0, 0.6, 0.7, 0.8, 0.9 and 1.0) have been investigated, and great improvement in luminescence was obtained. Their structures and corresponding characteristics were controlled systematically by the cation exchange of Ca2+ ions with Sr2+ ions, exhibiting the influences on the charge balance by generating a rearrangement of nitride clusters in the host lattice as well as demonstrating the impacts on various sites in the nitride matrix. The unit cell expansion, enhancement of the bc plane, and adjustment of the a axis were realized through the cation substitution in SRCAANES. A large amount of high-coordination sites was formed in the Sr/Eu sites due to lattice distortion and sufficient interspace was provided for charge balance of activators in a reduced environment, as analyzed by XANES (X-ray absorption near-edge structure spectroscopy). A more than 10% enhancement in PL intensity and a blue shift of emission peaks were also achieved in the substituted samples. Additionally, HRTCEM (high-resolution transmission electron microscopy) further illustrated the effects of cation exchange on their microstructure. Solid-state Raman spectra demonstrated the changes in the anion environment, caused by a special kind of cation exchange in the nitride system, as well as the characteristic ordering variations of SiN4 and AlN4 units in the matrix. Finally, sequences of (Si/Al)N4 around the divalent sites were further investigated using solid-state 23Na and 27Al nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy. These ordering distributions at the activator sites were responsible for the rigid structure of the host matrix and consequently the enhancement of the thermal quenching characteristics. Thermal stability at 473 K was increased by 10% when x=0.9 in SRCAANES:Eu2+ compared with x=0.0. Thus, the exploration of the neighbor sequence for selective tetrahedral sites (Li2+, Mg2+, Al3+ and Si4+) coordinated by N atoms in contact with the cation sites in a nitride host has been initiated.
